data.title: Cactus Insider Selling Raises Questions On Confidence And Shareholder Alignment
data.description:
• Cactus (NYSE:WHD) executives Joel Bender and Scott Bender have disclosed significant insider stock sales in March 2026.
  • The President and the Chairman/CEO together sold more than 200,000 shares, according to recent SEC filings.
  • There have been no insider purchases reported at Cactus over the past year, highlighting a one sided pattern of insider activity.

Cactus, which trades on the NYSE under the ticker WHD, last closed at $46.36. The stock is down 7.3% over the past week and 19.1% over the past month, while still showing a gain of 59.1% over the past five years. For shareholders, these insider moves are landing against a backdrop of recent share price pressure, but a longer term track record of positive returns.

For you as an investor, concentrated insider selling without any offsetting buying can be a useful piece of information when you think about leadership incentives and confidence. It does not tell the whole story by itself, but when combined with the recent share price moves and your own view of Cactus fundamentals, it may help you decide how closely to monitor future filings and management commentary.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Cactus by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Cactus.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Cactus following this latest news.

The twin sales by Joel and Scott Bender sit at the intersection of insider behavior and a stock that has recently come under price pressure but still has a longer term gain. When two top executives with long histories at the company reduce their holdings by more than 200,000 shares in a short window, and there are no reported insider buys over the past year, it often reads as a cooling of insider enthusiasm rather than a high conviction stance on future upside. That said, insider selling can be driven by many personal reasons that are not visible to outside investors, so it is usually better treated as a sentiment signal than a standalone thesis.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Cactus Narrative

  • The recent sales come after Cactus reported Q4 2025 results that were ahead of analyst expectations and after several brokers raised their price targets. This is consistent with the narrative that management has created value through strong execution, cost discipline and acquisitions.
  • At the same time, concentrated selling by both the President and CEO sits uncomfortably next to a narrative that leans on long term expansion, including Baker Hughes integration and Middle East growth, and may make some investors question how confident insiders are about that path.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on earnings, margins, acquisitions and industry demand, while this pattern of insider activity, including five sales and no buys over the past year, is a governance and signaling factor that is not fully captured in those operational assumptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Cactus to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A one sided pattern of insider selling over the past year, with no reported purchases, can point to reduced alignment between insiders and outside shareholders if it continues.
  • ⚠️ Weak near and mid term sentiment signals, plus share price sensitivity to moves in crude oil and natural gas, mean that further commodity swings or softer activity could keep the stock volatile.
  • 🎁 Cactus has reported profitability and Q4 2025 results that exceeded analyst expectations on both revenue and adjusted EPS, which some investors may see as operational strength despite recent selling.
  • 🎁 Brokers such as Citi, Barclays and Stifel have recently lifted their price targets following those results, supporting the view that Cactus remains a relevant player in oilfield equipment alongside peers like Baker Hughes, Schlumberger and Halliburton.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on whether insider activity broadens out, for example if other executives or directors also sell or if any insider buying appears that might rebalance the picture. Track future SEC filings next to quarterly results to see if selling clusters around earnings strength or stock price spikes, and watch how the market reacts to any further moves in energy prices, given the recent share price reaction to crude and gas. You may also want to see whether management commentary on calls addresses capital allocation, personal shareholdings or incentives in a way that either reinforces or softens the signal from these transactions.

To ensure you're always in the loop on how the latest news impacts the investment narrative for Cactus, head to the community page for Cactus to never miss an update on the top community narratives.

data.title: Is Alaska Air Group (ALK) Pricing Make Sense After Recent Share Price Slide
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Alaska Air Group is a bargain or a value trap at around US$38 per share, you are not alone, and the answer depends on how you look at its valuation.
  • The stock has seen sharp moves recently, with a 10.2% decline over the last 7 days, a 33.9% decline over 30 days, and a 26.2% decline year to date, on top of a 29.6% decline over the past year and 46.3% over five years.
  • These price swings have kept Alaska Air Group on many investors' watchlists, as they reassess what the current share price implies about the business. Recent news has focused on the company as part of broader discussions about US airlines, their cost pressures, and changing travel patterns, which all feed into how the market currently prices the stock.
  • On our valuation checks, Alaska Air Group scores just 1 out of 6 for being potentially undervalued. Next, we will look at what different valuation methods say about that score and then finish with a framework that can help you make even more sense of those numbers.

Alaska Air Group scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Alaska Air Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projections of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the whole business might be worth right now.

For Alaska Air Group, the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model uses recent Free Cash Flow of a loss of about $457.7 million and projects this turning into positive Free Cash Flow of $190.1 million by 2028. Beyond the initial analyst estimates, Simply Wall St extrapolates additional cash flows out to 2035, all expressed in US$, to build a full picture of possible future cash generation.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $8.49 per share. Compared with the current share price of around $38, this specific DCF output suggests the stock is very expensive relative to those cash flow assumptions, with an implied overvaluation of around 347.7%.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Alaska Air Group may be overvalued by 347.7%. Discover 48 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Alaska Air Group.

Approach 2: Alaska Air Group Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a common way to think about valuation because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that support that share. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how much risk they see in the business, so there is no single “right” number that fits every company.

Alaska Air Group currently trades on a P/E of 43.56x. That sits well above the Airlines industry average of 8.40x and also above the peer group average of 20.52x, which suggests the market is placing a richer price on its earnings than on many other airline stocks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a more tailored P/E might look like for Alaska Air Group, given factors such as its earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk characteristics. Because it blends all of these into one figure, it is often more useful than a simple comparison with peers or a broad industry average. For Alaska Air Group, the Fair Ratio is 43.32x, which is very close to the current P/E of 43.56x, so the shares look priced at about the level this model would suggest.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Alaska Air Group Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St you can use Narratives, where you connect your own story about Alaska Air Group to a set of numbers, build a forecast for revenue, earnings and margins, see a Fair Value, and then compare it with the current price. Each Narrative lives on the Community page, updating as new news or earnings land, and often looking quite different from one investor who sees Alaska Air Group closer to US$86.64 per share and another who sees it nearer to US$49 per share.
